title: Hand shearing a small flock
subtitle: Using blade shears on up to a dozen sheep, following the traditional British shearing sequence.
excerpt: Hand shears are slower than electric clippers but quieter, lighter, and better suited to a small flock with no power at the field gate. The technique is a sequence of long blows working across the body in a defined order so every part of the sheep is accessible without repositioning more than necessary. A competent shearer can complete a sheep in eight to twelve minutes with blades.
sourceNotes:
British Wool, training and shearing guides. https://www.britishwool.org.uk
DEFRA, Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Livestock: Sheep (1998). https://www.gov.uk

content.text: The right moment to shear is when the

is clearly established, usually late May to June in most UK regions. Part the fleece near the shoulder and look at the base: if new white fibres are visible lifting the old fleece, the rise is in. Shearing before the rise puts the blades against the skin and makes nicks more likely; shearing too late loses the new growth and traps moisture under the fleece.
Keep sheep off grass and dry for twelve hours before shearing. Wet wool is nearly impossible to shear with hand blades and the damp fleece is useless for sale.
heavily soiled breech areas before setting the sheep up.

Sit the sheep on its rump, balanced on the tail, leaning slightly back between your knees. The head rests on the side of your knee. Hold the head gently against your leg with your forearm while you work. The sheep should be still; a struggling sheep is usually improperly balanced.
Begin on the belly and inside of the right thigh, then the right side of the neck. Work across the left shoulder and down the left flank in long

parallel to the backbone. Reposition to the left side and work the left flank. Finish with the back and breech. The skin should stay taut under the blades: use your free hand to pull the skin smooth ahead of the cut.

Skin cuts are inevitable when learning to shear. For a small nick, spray immediately with antiseptic. Do not try to stitch a shearing cut: the wound is clean and will close on its own if kept dry. A cut on the teats of a lactating ewe needs veterinary attention if it is more than superficial. Note every nick in the animal's record.
